stem: Aisha earns $\$1{,}200$ per week. Her weekly expenses are: rent $\$550$, groceries $\$120$, transport $\$85$, utilities $\$45$, and phone $\$30$. Calculate her weekly surplus.
hint: Weekly surplus $=$ weekly income $-$ total weekly expenses.

stem: Calculate the total weekly expenses for a household with the following costs: rent $\$490$, food $\$135$, transport $\$80$, streaming subscriptions $\$25$, and gym membership $\$20$.
hint: Add all weekly expense amounts together.

stem: Ben earns $\$1{,}050$ per week. His total weekly expenses are $\$780$. Calculate his weekly surplus.
hint: Weekly surplus $=$ weekly income $-$ total weekly expenses.

stem: Chloe earns $\$1{,}450$ per week. Her weekly expenses are: mortgage $\$680$, utilities $\$90$, groceries $\$160$, transport $\$95$, phone $\$40$, and health insurance $\$55$. Calculate her weekly surplus.
hint: Add all expenses first, then subtract from weekly income.

stem: Daniel's monthly budget includes the following fixed expenses: rent $\$1{,}400$, phone $\$65$, internet $\$75$, car insurance $\$120$, and health insurance $\$95$. Calculate his total monthly fixed expenses.
hint: Fixed expenses are costs that do not change from month to month. Add all fixed items.

stem: Emma has a monthly income of $\$5{,}200$. Her total monthly expenses are $\$4{,}160$. Calculate her monthly savings rate as a percentage of her income.

A household budget has the following monthly categories: mortgage $\$1{,}850$ (fixed), groceries $\$560$ (necessary), dining out $\$420$ (discretionary), transport $\$200$ (mostly fixed), and streaming services $\$30$ (fixed). Which category has the most room for reduction without affecting essential needs?
hint: Consider which expenses are fixed, essential, or discretionary.
explanation: The mortgage, transport and streaming services are largely fixed and cannot easily be reduced. Groceries are an essential cost. Dining out ($\$420$) is a discretionary expense — it can be reduced without affecting essential needs and is the largest variable category available.

stem: Calculate the total monthly variable expenses from the following budget items: groceries $\$420$, entertainment $\$180$, dining out $\$120$, clothing $\$85$, and personal care $\$65$.
hint: Variable expenses are costs that can change from month to month. Add all variable items.

stem: Priya earns $\$4{,}800$ per month. Her total monthly expenses are $\$3{,}600$. She wants to save a 3-month emergency fund. How many months will it take her to reach this goal?
hint: Emergency fund $= 3 \times$ monthly expenses. Months $= \dfrac{\text{emergency fund}}{\text{monthly surplus}}$.

stem: Liam earns $\$6{,}000$ per month. His fixed monthly expenses total $\$2{,}400$ and his variable monthly expenses total $\$1{,}600$. How many months will it take him to save a 3-month emergency fund?
hint: First find total expenses and monthly surplus, then calculate the emergency fund target.

stem: Mia has a monthly surplus of $\$800$ and wants to save a 3-month emergency fund of $\$9{,}600$. At her current rate this will take 12 months. Which single budget change would allow her to reach her goal in the fewest months?
options: Reduce dining out by $\$200$ per month
Switch to a cheaper gym membership, saving $\$15$ per month
Buy groceries on special, saving $\$25$ per month
answer: Reduce dining out by $\$200$ per month
hint: Calculate the new monthly surplus for each option, then find how many months $\dfrac{\$9{,}600}{\text{new surplus}}$ takes.
explanation: Reducing dining out by $\$200$ gives a new surplus of $\$1{,}000$, so months $= \dfrac{9{,}600}{1{,}000} = 9.6$, approximately 10 months. The other options increase the surplus by at most $\$25$, leaving the number of months unchanged at 12. Reducing dining out gives the largest surplus increase and the fastest result.

stem: Noah's monthly income is $\$4{,}000$ and his total monthly expenses are $\$3{,}200$. If he reduces his entertainment spending by $\$160$ per month, how many months will it take him to save a 3-month emergency fund?
hint: Find the new monthly surplus after the reduction, then divide the emergency fund target by this amount.
explanation: Original surplus $= 4{,}000 - 3{,}200 = \$800$. After reducing entertainment: new surplus $= 800 + 160 = \$960$. Emergency fund $= 3 \times 3{,}200 = \$9{,}600$. Months $= \dfrac{9{,}600}{960} = 10$.
